quizQuestion: Going down the halogen group (HF → HCl → HBr → HI), binary acid strength increases because ___ decreases.
explanation: As you move down a group, the halogen atom gets larger, making the H–X bond longer and weaker. A weaker bond means H+ is released more easily, so acid strength increases: HF << HCl < HBr < HI. Note that across a period, electronegativity dominates instead.
statement: Binary acid strength increases down a group (HF < HCl < HBr < HI) because H–X bond strength decreases with increasing atomic size.
wowFactor: HF is actually one of the weakest hydrohalic acids despite fluorine being the most electronegative element — the F–H bond is so strong it barely ionizes at all.
visualDescription: A vertical periodic table column showing the four hydrogen halides with bond length arrows increasing and acid strength arrows pointing upward, on a clean white background
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: For the chlorine oxyacids (HClO < HClO2 < HClO3 < HClO4), acid strength increases with ___.
explanation: Each additional oxygen atom draws electron density away from the O–H bond through induction, weakening it and making H+ easier to release. HClO4 (perchloric acid) is one of the strongest known acids because four oxygens pull so effectively on the O–H bond.
statement: Oxyacid strength increases with more oxygen atoms: HClO < HClO2 < HClO3 < HClO4, because each O withdraws electron density and weakens the O–H bond.
wowFactor: Perchloric acid (HClO4) is so strong it essentially fully ionizes in water AND can even protonate other strong acids — a superacid precursor.
visualDescription: Four molecular diagrams of HClO, HClO2, HClO3, and HClO4 in a row with an upward arrow labeled 'acid strength' showing the trend, on a light gray background
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: higher electronegativity of the central atom
distractors: fewer lone pairs on central atom
lower oxidation state of central atom
quizQuestion: When comparing oxyacids with the same number of oxygen atoms, the stronger acid has a ___.
explanation: A more electronegative central atom pulls electron density away from the O–H bond more effectively, making the proton easier to release. For example, HOCl is a stronger acid than HOBr because Cl is more electronegative than Br.
statement: For oxyacids with equal oxygen counts, the stronger acid has a more electronegative central atom, which better withdraws electron density from the O–H bond.
wowFactor: This inductive effect cascades through bonds — a central atom three bonds away from the O–H can still meaningfully affect acid strength.
visualDescription: Side-by-side structural diagrams of HOCl and HOBr with electronegativity values labeled on the central atoms, and an arrow pointing to HOCl as the stronger acid
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: Which hydrogen halide is classified as a weak acid despite fluorine being the most electronegative halogen?
explanation: Fluorine's extreme electronegativity makes the H–F bond very short and very strong (bond energy ~569 kJ/mol vs. ~431 kJ/mol for H–Cl). This strong bond is so hard to break that HF is only partially ionized in water, making it a weak acid with Ka ≈ 6.8 × 10⁻⁴.
statement: HF is a weak acid (Ka ≈ 6.8 × 10⁻⁴) because the very strong H–F bond resists ionization, despite fluorine being the most electronegative element.
wowFactor: HF is so unusual that it forms bifluoride ions (HF2⁻) in solution — a hydrogen bond so strong it's almost a real chemical bond.
visualDescription: A simple molecular diagram of H–F with a bold bond line and 'weak acid' label, contrasted with H–Cl showing a longer, weaker bond labeled 'strong acid'
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: The mathematical definition of pKa is ___.
explanation: Just as pH = -log[H+], the pKa converts the acid dissociation constant Ka into a more workable logarithmic scale. A lower pKa means a larger Ka, which means a stronger acid. Acetic acid has Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ and pKa = 4.74.
statement: pKa = -log(Ka); a lower pKa value indicates a stronger acid with a larger dissociation constant.
wowFactor: The 'p' in pKa, pH, and pOH all come from the German word 'Potenz' (power), introduced by Søren Sørensen in 1909 when he invented the pH scale.
visualDescription: A clean equation graphic showing pKa = -log(Ka) with an arrow indicating 'stronger acid → lower pKa' on a white background with blue chemistry styling
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: the pH equals the pKa of the weak acid
quizQuestion: At the half-equivalence point in a weak acid titration, ___ is true.
explanation: At the half-equivalence point, exactly half the weak acid has been neutralized, so [A⁻] = [HA]. Plugging into Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log(1) = pKa + 0 = pKa. This is a direct experimental method to determine the pKa of any weak acid.
statement: At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration, [A⁻] = [HA], so pH = pKa exactly.
wowFactor: Chemists exploit this trick to experimentally measure pKa values — add half the stoichiometric amount of base, measure the pH, and you have the pKa.
visualDescription: A titration curve with the half-equivalence point marked at the midpoint of the buffering region, with a horizontal dashed line at pH = pKa and a vertical dashed line at half-volume
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: When a weak acid solution is diluted with water, its ___ even though Ka stays constant.
explanation: Dilution shifts equilibrium toward products (Le Chatelier's principle). Although the absolute [H+] decreases, the fraction of acid molecules that ionize increases. Ka = [H+][A⁻]/[HA] is constant, but with lower total acid concentration, a higher percentage must ionize to maintain Ka.
statement: Diluting a weak acid increases its percent ionization because equilibrium shifts toward products, even though Ka remains constant.
wowFactor: At infinite dilution, even the weakest acid would be 100% ionized — concentration is what keeps most of a weak acid in its molecular form.
visualDescription: Two beakers side by side showing a concentrated weak acid solution with few ions, and a dilute solution with a higher fraction of ions, illustrating the dilution effect on ionization
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: An acid with pKa = 3.5 compared to one with pKa = 6.0 is a ___.
explanation: A lower pKa corresponds to a higher Ka, meaning greater ionization and a stronger acid. pKa = 3.5 means Ka ≈ 3.2 × 10⁻⁴, while pKa = 6.0 means Ka = 1 × 10⁻⁶. The acid with pKa 3.5 ionizes about 320 times more.
statement: Lower pKa = higher Ka = stronger acid; an acid with pKa 3.5 is much stronger than one with pKa 6.0.
wowFactor: Each unit decrease in pKa represents a 10-fold increase in Ka — so pKa 3 is 1,000 times stronger than pKa 6.
visualDescription: A number line showing pKa values from 0 to 14, with 'stronger acid' labeled on the left (low pKa) and 'weaker acid' on the right (high pKa), with example acids marked
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: one unit above and below pKa
within one pH unit of pKa
quizQuestion: The effective buffering range of a weak acid/conjugate base buffer system is ___.
explanation: From Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). The buffer is effective when [A⁻]/[HA] is between 0.1 and 10, which corresponds to log values of -1 and +1, giving a pH range of pKa ± 1. Outside this range, the buffer is overwhelmed.
statement: A buffer is effective in the pH range of pKa ± 1, where the [A⁻]/[HA] ratio stays between 0.1 and 10.
wowFactor: Human blood uses a carbonic acid/bicarbonate buffer with pKa = 6.1, yet blood pH must stay between 7.35–7.45 — this is outside the 'optimal' range, relying on the lungs and kidneys for additional regulation.
visualDescription: A titration curve with the buffering plateau highlighted in green, spanning one pH unit above and below the pKa, with the pKa marked at the center
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: converts added H+ or OH- into the weak acid or conjugate base
acceptableAlternatives: absorbs H+ or OH- using the buffer components
reacts added H+ with A- or added OH- with HA
distractors: completely neutralizes all acid or base added
increases the volume to dilute any acid or base
uses the solvent to absorb changes
maintains a constant [H+] by suppressing ionization
stores acid or base for later release
quizQuestion: A buffer resists pH changes by ___.
explanation: When H+ is added, it reacts with A⁻ (the conjugate base): H+ + A⁻ → HA. When OH⁻ is added, it reacts with HA (the weak acid): OH⁻ + HA → A⁻ + H₂O. Both reactions consume the added acid or base and replenish the other buffer component, keeping the ratio [A⁻]/[HA] — and therefore pH — nearly constant.
statement: Buffers resist pH changes by reacting added H+ with the conjugate base (A⁻) or added OH⁻ with the weak acid (HA), converting them without significantly changing the pH.
wowFactor: The buffer in your blood can neutralize a full can of cola's worth of acid before your blood pH would shift dangerously — the bicarbonate system is remarkably robust.
visualDescription: A diagram showing two parallel reaction arrows: H+ + A⁻ → HA (top), and OH⁻ + HA → A⁻ + H₂O (bottom), with a double-headed arrow between them labeled 'buffer equilibrium'
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: pH equals pKa of the weak acid
quizQuestion: When a buffer is prepared with equal concentrations of weak acid and conjugate base ([A⁻] = [HA]), the resulting pH is ___.
explanation: Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]) = pKa + log(1) = pKa + 0 = pKa. Equal concentrations maximize buffering capacity symmetrically — the buffer can absorb equal amounts of added acid or base.
statement: When [A⁻] = [HA] in a buffer, pH = pKa. This is the optimal buffering condition with maximum symmetrical capacity.
wowFactor: Biochemists preparing enzyme assays meticulously choose buffer components so that [A⁻] = [HA] at physiological pH, giving the widest possible pH stability window.
visualDescription: A balance scale with [HA] on one side and [A⁻] on the other, perfectly level, with 'pH = pKa' labeled at the fulcrum
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: A buffer is considered effective when the [A⁻]/[HA] ratio is ___.
explanation: From Henderson-Hasselbalch, log([A⁻]/[HA]) must be between -1 and +1 for the buffer to function within 1 pH unit of pKa. This corresponds to [A⁻]/[HA] between 0.1 and 10. Outside this range, one component is nearly exhausted and the buffer fails.
statement: A buffer is effective when 0.1 ≤ [A⁻]/[HA] ≤ 10, keeping pH within the range pKa ± 1.
wowFactor: The 0.1–10 rule means you can use a 10-to-1 excess of either component and still have a working buffer — it's a surprisingly forgiving system.
visualDescription: A number line showing [A-]/[HA] ratios from 0.01 to 100, with the green 'effective' zone highlighted between 0.1 and 10, and red zones outside this range
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for calculating buffer pH is ___.
explanation: This equation is derived from Ka = [H+][A⁻]/[HA] by taking -log of both sides. It allows direct calculation of buffer pH from known pKa and the ratio of conjugate base to weak acid concentrations, without solving a quadratic equation.
statement: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]), used to calculate the pH of buffer solutions.
wowFactor: Lawrence Henderson derived this equation in 1908 to describe blood's bicarbonate buffer, and Karl Hasselbalch rewrote it in logarithmic form in 1916 — it's now used billions of times in labs worldwide.
visualDescription: A clean chalkboard-style graphic showing pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]) in large white chalk lettering, with labels identifying each component
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: conjugate base concentration decreases and weak acid concentration increases
quizQuestion: When a small amount of strong acid is added to a buffer, it ___.
explanation: The added H+ reacts with the conjugate base: H+ + A⁻ → HA. This consumes A⁻ (decreasing [A⁻]) and produces HA (increasing [HA]). The ratio [A⁻]/[HA] decreases slightly, so pH drops slightly but not drastically.
statement: Adding acid to a buffer converts A⁻ to HA: [A⁻] decreases and [HA] increases, causing a slight pH decrease according to Henderson-Hasselbalch.
wowFactor: A well-designed buffer barely flinches when acid is added — the pH might drop by 0.1 units when the same acid would drop an unbuffered solution by 5 or more units.
visualDescription: A reaction diagram showing H+ ions attacking A⁻ molecules to form HA, with a downward pH arrow showing a slight decrease on the pH scale
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: conjugate base concentration increases and weak acid concentration decreases
quizQuestion: When a small amount of strong base is added to a buffer, it ___.
explanation: The added OH⁻ reacts with the weak acid: OH⁻ + HA → A⁻ + H₂O. This consumes HA (decreasing [HA]) and produces A⁻ (increasing [A⁻]). The ratio [A⁻]/[HA] increases slightly, so pH rises slightly but not drastically.
statement: Adding base to a buffer converts HA to A⁻: [A⁻] increases and [HA] decreases, causing a slight pH increase according to Henderson-Hasselbalch.
wowFactor: This is why biochemists can add NaOH dropwise to enzymatic reactions without destroying the experiment — the buffer soaks it up silently.
visualDescription: A reaction diagram showing OH⁻ reacting with HA to form A⁻ and water, with an upward pH arrow showing a slight increase on the pH scale
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

quizQuestion: In the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, when [A⁻] = [HA], the equation simplifies to ___.
explanation: When [A⁻] = [HA], log([A⁻]/[HA]) = log(1) = 0. Therefore pH = pKa + 0 = pKa. This elegant simplification is the foundation for using the half-equivalence point to experimentally determine pKa values.
statement: In Henderson-Hasselbalch, when [A⁻] = [HA], log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa + 0 = pKa.
wowFactor: This simplification is so useful that AP exam problems frequently test it — if you see equal concentrations of acid and conjugate base, the pH answer is always pKa.
visualDescription: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation with [A-]/[HA] = 1 substituted in, showing the log(1) = 0 step and the final result pH = pKa, all in clean equation form
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: amount of acid or base a buffer can absorb before pH changes significantly
acceptableAlternatives: amount of strong acid or base the buffer can neutralize
moles of H+ or OH- a buffer can absorb
how much acid or base a buffer can handle
distractors: the pH range over which a buffer is effective
the concentration of the weak acid alone
the Ka of the weak acid
the total volume of the buffer solution
the number of buffer components present
the pKa of the buffer system
the maximum pH the buffer can reach
quizQuestion: Buffer capacity is defined as the ___.
explanation: Buffer capacity is a quantitative measure of how much strong acid or base a buffer can absorb before the pH changes by more than 1 unit. It depends on the total concentration of buffer components (not just the ratio). A 1 M acetate buffer has much greater capacity than a 0.01 M one, even if both have pH = pKa.
statement: Buffer capacity is the amount of strong acid or base a buffer can absorb before its pH changes significantly, determined primarily by total buffer concentration.
wowFactor: Olympic-grade blood doping with bicarbonate ('soda loading') exploits buffer capacity — athletes ingest sodium bicarbonate to increase blood buffer capacity and delay lactic acid buildup.
visualDescription: Two containers of buffer solution — one concentrated (tall, dark) and one dilute (short, light) — with different amounts of acid dropping in, illustrating that the concentrated one absorbs more before the pH arrow shifts
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: greater total concentration of buffer components
increased total molarity of weak acid and conjugate base
distractors: equal volumes of acid and conjugate base
quizQuestion: Two buffers have the same pKa and the same [A⁻]/[HA] ratio, but different total concentrations. Greater buffer capacity belongs to the buffer with ___.
explanation: Buffer capacity depends on how many moles of buffer components are present. If total concentration doubles (both [HA] and [A⁻] double), the buffer can absorb twice as many moles of added acid or base. The pH and ratio stay the same, but the capacity doubles.
statement: Buffer capacity increases with total buffer concentration — twice as much weak acid and conjugate base can neutralize twice as much added strong acid or base.
wowFactor: This is why IV saline solutions used in hospitals contain carefully optimized concentrations — too dilute and the buffer fails, too concentrated and osmotic pressure problems arise.
visualDescription: A side-by-side comparison of two buffer beakers with identical ratios but different concentrations, each with a capacity gauge (like a fuel gauge) showing the more concentrated one at full capacity
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

acceptableAlternatives: when the concentrations of conjugate base and weak acid are equal
distractors: when the buffer is most dilute
when all weak acid has been consumed
quizQuestion: Buffer capacity is at its maximum ___.
explanation: When [A⁻] = [HA], the buffer has equal reserves of both components. It can absorb equal amounts of added acid (using A⁻) or added base (using HA) before one component is depleted. If [A⁻] >> [HA], the buffer handles base poorly; if [HA] >> [A⁻], it handles acid poorly.
statement: Maximum buffer capacity occurs when [A⁻] = [HA] (pH = pKa), giving the buffer symmetric and maximum resistance to both added acid and base.
wowFactor: Biological buffers are often tuned to operate slightly away from pKa because the ratio of products to reactants is rarely 1:1 in living systems — nature prioritizes function over theoretical maximum.
visualDescription: A symmetrical diagram showing a buffer at [A-] = [HA] at center, with equal arrows pointing left (handles acid) and right (handles base), compared to an asymmetrical buffer that handles one direction much better
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8

correctAnswer: all of the weak acid or all of the conjugate base is consumed
acceptableAlternatives: one buffer component is completely depleted
distractors: the Ka of the buffer changes
the pKa shifts by more than 1 unit
more than 50% of HA has ionized
the buffer is diluted beyond 0.001 M
quizQuestion: Buffer capacity is exceeded and pH begins to change dramatically when ___.
explanation: If enough acid is added, all the A⁻ is converted to HA and there is no conjugate base left to react with further H+. Similarly, if enough base is added, all HA is converted to A⁻ and no weak acid remains. At that point, added acid or base acts on the solution like a simple unbuffered system.
statement: Buffer capacity is exceeded when all of one component (HA or A⁻) is consumed — after that, added acid or base causes large, rapid pH changes.
wowFactor: This failure point appears graphically as the sudden steep rise or fall at the ends of a titration curve's buffering plateau — the visual 'cliff' marks where the buffer gave up.
visualDescription: A titration curve showing the flat buffering plateau collapsing into a steep pH rise at the equivalence point, with an arrow marking 'buffer capacity exceeded' at the inflection where the slope suddenly increases
sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED Unit 8
